title: Stellar J-Harvesting: a novel angular momentum technosignature and first search in the Kepler field
summary: We introduce Stellar J-Harvesting (SJH), a technosignature in which an engineered system extracts rotational angular momentum from a star. Unlike a classical Dyson sphere, such a system need not produce a detectable mid-infrared excess; its clearest signature would be a star rotating more slowly than comparable peers. We derive the energy-period relation for this effect, outline several possible coupling channels, and apply a population-relative slow-rotator search to Kepler stars using colour-gravity bins. After eight false-positive filters, the clean sample contains 6,725 FGK main-sequence stars. We find two >4 sigma slow rotators, but Gaia DR3 indicators and WISE imaging point to more ordinary explanations, including unresolved binaries and low metallicity. We therefore make no detection claim and place a conservative pilot upper limit on the occurrence of strong SJH-like signals, f_SJH < 4.5e-4. The main result is a search framework: angular-momentum technosignatures are testable with existing stellar-rotation catalogues, and the strongest outliers define concrete targets for spectroscopic, imaging, and radio follow-up.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Stellar J-Harvesting (SJH), a technosignature where an engineered system extracts rotational angular momentum from a star, searches Kepler stars for anomalously slow rotators, finds two >4σ outliers but attributes them to natural causes, and reports a framework and upper limit (f_SJH < 4.5e-4) for future searches.
AI.motivation: To propose a new class of technosignature (angular-momentum harvesting) distinct from traditional Dyson-sphere infrared searches, and to demonstrate its testability using existing stellar rotation catalogues.
AI.method: Derived the energy-period relation for SJH, outlined coupling channels, applied a population-relative slow-rotator search on 6,725 FGK Kepler main-sequence stars using colour-gravity bins, and applied eight false-positive filters.
AI.result: Two >4σ slow rotators were found, but Gaia DR3 and WISE data indicate natural explanations (unresolved binaries, low metallicity). No detection claimed; a conservative upper limit f_SJH < 4.5e-4 was placed.
AI.conclusion: Angular-momentum technosignatures are testable with current catalogues; the search framework identifies concrete targets for multi-wavelength follow-up.

title: Physics of Eclipsing Binaries. VI. Hot, compact stars
comment: 13 pages, 9 figures. Accepted for publication in ApJS
summary: Models of eclipsing binaries require the assignment of appropriate emergent intensities to the surface elements of the binary components. For distance-dependent modelling of flux-calibrated light curves, this necessitates an approximation of the absolute normal intensities of both components of the binary, as well as how their brightness varies across the stellar disks (limb darkening). Such surface intensities are often inferred from other physical properties of the synthetic binary (effective temperature, surface gravity, etc.) through the use of model atmospheres, which in turn are generally suited to a particular range of stellar types or parameters. Here, we present the major developments included in the PHOEBE 2.5 release (publicly available from http://phoebe-project.org), which improve the fidelity of model binaries comprising hot, compact stars. These developments include the incorporation model atmospheres produced using the Tubingen Model Atmosphere Package (TMAP) and Montreal/Tremblay codes (complementing the already incorporated PHOENIX and Castelli & Kurucz models, primarily suited to main sequences stars and low-temperature giants). Similarly, PHOEBE v2.5 now allows for blending/extrapolation of model atmospheres, meaning one can continue to make use of model atmospheres in cases when a small number of surface elements have parameters outside the model atmosphere grid. As an added value product, we also present tables of limb-darkening coefficients derived from the newly incorporated model atmospheres, such that they can be used as inputs in other binary modelling codes.

AI.tldr: PHOEBE 2.5 introduces new model atmospheres (TMAP, Montreal/Tremblay) for hot compact stars, atmosphere blending/extrapolation, and derived limb-darkening coefficient tables.
AI.motivation: Existing PHOEBE models (PHOENIX, Castelli & Kurucz) are suited mainly to main sequence stars and cool giants, lacking fidelity for hot, compact stars (e.g., white dwarfs, hot subdwarfs).
AI.method: Incorporation of TMAP and Montreal/Tremblay model atmospheres; implementation of blending/extrapolation for surface elements outside grid parameter space; derivation of limb-darkening coefficients from new atmospheres.
AI.result: PHOEBE 2.5 extends reliable binary modelling to hot compact stars; enables atmosphere blending/extrapolation for edge-case surface elements; provides pre-computed limb-darkening tables as a community resource.
AI.conclusion: The updates significantly improve the fidelity and applicability range of PHOEBE for eclipsing binary modelling, particularly for systems containing hot, compact components.

title: Node-locked phase of annual modulations from the gravitational chiral anomaly in the solar Kerr field
summary: The Chern-Pontryagin density of the Sun's Kerr field, ${}^*RR\simeq 288\,G^{2}M_{\odot}^{2}a_{\odot}\cosθ/c^{4}r^{7}$, provides an annually sign-changing parity-odd curvature invariant sampled by a detector in the Solar System and sources the gravitational chiral anomaly $\nabla_μj^μ_{5}={}^*RR/384π^{2}$. Sampled along the Earth's orbit it is odd about the solar equatorial plane, so the accumulated chirality is extremal at the plane crossings. Any observable coupled to this reservoir therefore modulates annually with a phase fixed entirely by ephemerides: the cosine-fit phase is $t^{*}=158.7$ d (June 7-8) for a rate-suppressing coupling or $t^{*}=341.4$ d (December 7-8) for a rate-enhancing one, with a calculable secular drift of $+0.014$ d yr$^{-1}$, strict energy independence, a phase-locked semiannual harmonic of $3.75\%$, and no synodic (27 d) or solar-cycle (11 yr) components. The sign and magnitude of the coupling are not predicted; the phase is. A single-amplitude fit to the DAMA/LIBRA-phase2 residuals selects the suppressing branch and describes the observed modulation as well as the standard-halo dark-matter cosine ($Δχ^{2}=1.8$). The most precise published phase, from the full 3.40 t yr exposure, $t^{*}=153.5\pm3.8$ d, lies $0.3σ$ from the halo value and $1.4σ$ from the node-locked one: the $6.2$-day separation between the hypotheses is below the current resolution. Unlike the halo phase, the node-locked phase admits no astrophysical adjustment, so phase metrology at the two-day level, and a test of its energy independence, discriminates between the two hypotheses.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes that the Chern-Pontryagin density of the Sun's Kerr field creates an annually sign-changing parity-odd curvature invariant, which sources a gravitational chiral anomaly. The resulting modulation has a fixed phase (either June 7-8 for rate-suppressing or December 7-8 for rate-enhancing coupling), energy independence, and specific harmonic structure. The DAMA/LIBRA-phase2 data favors the suppressing branch, and phase metrology at two-day resolution can discriminate between this node-locked hypothesis and the standard halo dark matter model.
AI.motivation: To propose a novel, purely gravitational (non-particle dark matter) explanation for the annual modulation signal observed in DAMA/LIBRA, with a phase prediction fixed entirely by ephemerides rather than astrophysical assumptions, enabling clear observational discrimination from the standard halo dark matter hypothesis.
AI.method: The authors compute the Chern-Pontryagin density of the Sun's Kerr field, derive the gravitational chiral anomaly, sample the parity-odd curvature invariant along Earth's orbit, and fit the predicted modulation phase to DAMA/LIBRA-phase2 residuals. They compare the node-locked phase (fixed by solar system geometry) to the standard halo dark matter phase prediction.
AI.result: The node-locked phase predicts cosine-fit phases of 158.7 d (June 7-8) for rate-suppressing coupling or 341.4 d (December 7-8) for rate-enhancing coupling, with a secular drift of +0.014 d yr⁻¹, strict energy independence, a 3.75% semiannual harmonic, and no synodic or solar-cycle components. DAMA/LIBRA-phase2 data selects the suppressing branch (Δχ²=1.8 vs. halo model). The published phase 153.5±3.8 d lies 0.3σ from the halo value and 1.4σ from the node-locked value; the 6.2-day separation is below current resolution.
AI.conclusion: Phase metrology at the two-day level and tests of energy independence can discriminate between the node-locked (gravitational chiral anomaly) and standard halo dark matter hypotheses, as the node-locked phase admits no astrophysical adjustment unlike the halo phase.

title: Cluster-Weighted Training of Deep Surrogate Models for Subgrid Turbulent Transport
comment: 9 pages, 8 figures, 2 tables, SABID workshop at 2025 IEEE International Conference on Data Mining
summary: Turbulence in the solar interior and atmosphere plays a crucial role in energy transport, yet modeling its subgrid-scale effects remains a major challenge. This study leverages machine learning (ML) models to predict components of the Reynolds stress tensor using high-resolution StellarBox simulations of the quiet Sun. Previously, we have compared a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) and a 3D Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) against physics-based baselines to achieve a lower Mean Squared Error (MSE) and better generalization across various heights and depths in the solar atmosphere. To enhance learning, in this work, we investigate cluster-weighted training using K-Means and Hierarchical Agglomerative Clustering (HAC). By weighing the loss function based on cluster-specific prediction errors, we direct the model's attention to high-error regions. It significantly improves CNN performance, achieving 34% lower MSE and a significantly higher R2 score indicating that integrating deterministic clustering with ML is a promising technique for modeling subgrid turbulence, in particular, and regression in diverse environments, in general.

AI.tldr: This study uses cluster-weighted training (K-Means and HAC) to improve ML models (MLP and 3D CNN) for predicting Reynolds stress tensor components in solar turbulence simulations, achieving 34% lower MSE and higher R² scores.
AI.motivation: Modeling subgrid-scale turbulence in the solar interior and atmosphere is crucial for energy transport but remains a major challenge; traditional physics-based baselines perform poorly.
AI.method: Cluster-weighted training using K-Means and Hierarchical Agglomerative Clustering (HAC) to weight the loss function based on cluster-specific prediction errors, directing model attention to high-error regions, applied to MLP and 3D CNN.
AI.result: 34% lower MSE and significantly higher R² score for CNN performance compared to physics-based baselines and unweighted training.
AI.conclusion: Integrating deterministic clustering with ML is a promising technique for modeling subgrid turbulence and regression in diverse environments.

title: A Transport Theory of Turbulent Coronal Heating in General Geometry
summary: Magnetic geometry shapes how turbulence transports and dissipates energy in strongly magnetized plasmas. The solar corona, a maze of open and closed flux tubes with sharp transverse gradients, is a prominent example, yet most wave-turbulence models of coronal heating assume symmetric flux tubes or add geometric effects in ad hoc ways. Here we develop a geometry-complete multiscale transport theory for reduced-magnetohydrodynamic turbulence in an arbitrary background field, retaining squashing (magnetic shear), transverse gradients, curvature, and gravity at the same order as standard expansion-driven reflection, and coupling fast, anisotropic fluctuations to slow background evolution through conservation laws. Applied to the corona, it recovers the standard reflection-driven turbulent cascade in smooth regions such as coronal-hole interiors, but predicts that in structured regions geometry-driven channels can dominate: squashing drives reflection even when parallel Alfvén-speed gradients are weak; curvature and non-radial geometry drive compressive heating channels; and waves catalyze the relaxation of velocity shear into heat. The same dynamics drive cross-field transport of mass, composition, momentum, and heat across open-closed interfaces, at rates rivaling the field-parallel supply from the base. These effects bias heating to low altitudes in structured regions, giving a physical basis for the coronal-hole--boundary corrections used in empirical wind-speed predictors. Additionally, the framework's slow-timescale transport equations could be evolved in time, providing a route to a global, geometry-aware model of a structured wave-driven corona and wind. More broadly, the theory provides an energy-consistent account of turbulence, geometry, and transport effects relevant to various astrophysical and terrestrial settings, from magnetospheres and accretion flows to fusion experiments.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a geometry-complete multiscale transport theory for reduced-MHD turbulence in arbitrary magnetic fields, showing that in structured regions of the solar corona, geometry-driven channels (squashing, curvature, non-radial geometry) can dominate over standard reflection-driven cascades, with implications for coronal heating, cross-field transport, and wind-speed predictions.
AI.motivation: Existing wave-turbulence models of coronal heating assume symmetric flux tubes or add geometric effects in ad hoc ways, yet the solar corona consists of a complex maze of open and closed flux tubes with sharp transverse gradients. A geometry-complete theory is needed to capture how magnetic structure shapes turbulent energy transport and dissipation.
AI.method: The authors develop a multiscale transport theory for reduced-MHD turbulence in an arbitrary background field, retaining squashing (magnetic shear), transverse gradients, curvature, and gravity at the same order as standard expansion-driven reflection, and coupling fast anisotropic fluctuations to slow background evolution through conservation laws.
AI.result: In smooth regions (e.g., coronal-hole interiors), the standard reflection-driven turbulent cascade is recovered. In structured regions, geometry-driven channels dominate: squashing drives reflection even without parallel Alfvén-speed gradients; curvature and non-radial geometry drive compressive heating; waves catalyze velocity shear relaxation into heat. Cross-field transport of mass, composition, momentum, and heat across open-closed interfaces rivals field-parallel supply. These effects bias heating to low altitudes in structured regions.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a physical basis for coronal-hole boundary corrections in empirical wind-speed predictors, offers a route to global geometry-aware models of a structured wave-driven corona and wind, and gives an energy-consistent account of turbulence, geometry, and transport effects applicable to magnetospheres, accretion flows, and fusion experiments.

title: RedDots: Magnetic field of the nearby active M dwarf GJ 729, and a search for companions
summary: M dwarfs are prime targets for discovering exoplanets, and the nearest M dwarfs to the Sun provide among the best opportunities for follow-up detailed exoplanet characterization. GJ 729, the seventh closest M dwarf to the Sun, presents significant challenges for exoplanet detection due to its high levels of magnetic activity. To address this, we present a detailed analysis of GJ 729's magnetic field and its variability, followed by a search for exoplanets beneath the activity-induced noise in the stellar radial velocity. The geometry of GJ 729's large-scale magnetic field was reconstructed using new and archival spectropolarimetric data for a total of four epochs spanning 10 years. Results indicate a weak large-scale field ranging from 50 to 145 G, and an evolving non-axisymmetric field geometry that varies from poloidal dominated to a near-equal poloidal-toroidal configuration. We modeled activity-induced radial velocity variations using Gaussian Process Regression and activity diagnostics, and searched for planetary companions using ~90 d of high-cadence spectra taken contemporaneously with the high-precision CARMENES and HARPS spectrographs. Activity-only and activity + Keplerian models offered statistically equivalent fits, with a consistently preferred Keplerian period of ~7 d and amplitude of ~1.9 m/s across a range of activity modeling approaches. This could relate to an Earth-mass or Super-Earth planet, or residual stellar activity with power concentrated at a multiple of the rotation half-period. Our findings provide insight into the magnetic behavior of fully convective M dwarfs, and highlight the potential and challenges of detecting Keplerian RV signatures that are only a fraction of activity amplitudes.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes the magnetic activity of GJ 729 (the 7th closest M dwarf to the Sun) and searches for exoplanets beneath activity-induced radial velocity noise. Using spectropolarimetric data spanning 10 years and high-cadence spectra from CARMENES and HARPS, they find a weak evolving large-scale magnetic field (50–145 G) and a tentative ~7-day Keplerian signal (~1.9 m/s amplitude) that could be an Earth-mass/Super-Earth planet or residual stellar activity at a multiple of the rotation half-period.
AI.motivation: M dwarfs are prime exoplanet targets, but GJ 729, the 7th closest M dwarf, presents challenges for exoplanet detection due to high levels of magnetic activity. Understanding and disentangling activity-induced noise from genuine planetary signals is critical.
AI.method: Large-scale magnetic field geometry reconstructed from new and archival spectropolarimetric data across four epochs (10 years). Activity-induced RV variations modeled using Gaussian Process Regression with activity diagnostics. Planetary search conducted using ~90 days of high-cadence CARMENES and HARPS spectra, comparing activity-only vs. activity+Keplerian models.
AI.result: Weak large-scale field (50–145 G) with evolving non-axisymmetric geometry (poloidal-dominated to near-equal poloidal-toroidal). Activity-only and activity+Keplerian fits statistically equivalent, but a Keplerian period of ~7 days and amplitude of ~1.9 m/s is consistently preferred across modeling approaches.
AI.conclusion: The ~7-day signal could be an Earth-mass/Super-Earth planet or residual stellar activity at a multiple of the rotation half-period. Findings highlight the challenges of detecting Keplerian RV signatures at a fraction of activity amplitudes and provide insight into fully convective M dwarf magnetic behavior.

title: Investigation on the Relation between Active Regions' Compliance with Empirical Laws and Flare Productivity
summary: It remains evasive whether solar active regions (ARs) obeying or violating Hale's polarity law, Joy's tilt law, and the hemispheric helicity rule (HHR) differ in flare productivity. Here we conduct a comprehensive statistical analysis of ARs during the Solar Cycle 24 and the ascending phase of Cycle 25. ARs are automatically detected from full-disk line-of-sight magnetograms acquired by the Michelson Doppler Imager (MDI) and the Helioseismic and Magnetic Imager (HMI). We calculate tilt angles via flux-weighted polarity centroids, estimate magnetic twist by the force-free parameter $α_{\mathrm{best}}$ from HMI vector magnetograms, and measure flare productivity using the flare index (FI) built from GOES C-class-and-above events. Our results substantiate that the majority of ARs follow the aforementioned three empirical laws. The compliance rate tends to be higher for ARs emerging at higher latitudes or having larger centroid distance, while total unsigned magnetic flux exerts limited influence, with a clear positive correlation only for Hale's law. Overall, FI shows no significant discrepancies across different compliance groups, except that Cycle 24 ARs that satisfy Hale's and Joy's laws but violate the HHR exhibit higher FI than other groups. We also identify empirical thresholds for centroid distance and total unsigned flux, above which the median FI of binned ARs becomes nonzero. Combining the flux and distance thresholds effectively separates flare-productive from flare-quiet ARs. We hence conclude that the flare productivity of ARs is not dependent on the compliance with the empirical laws, but more closely associated with sufficiently large and strong magnetic systems.

AI.tldr: This study statistically analyzes solar active regions (ARs) from Solar Cycles 24-25, finding that while most ARs follow Hale's law, Joy's law, and the hemispheric helicity rule, flare productivity is not significantly correlated with compliance to these laws. Instead, flare productivity is more closely associated with large, magnetically strong systems, identified by thresholds in centroid distance and total unsigned flux.
AI.motivation: To determine whether solar active regions that obey or violate Hale's polarity law, Joy's tilt law, and the hemispheric helicity rule differ in flare productivity—a question that has remained elusive.
AI.method: Automatic detection of ARs from MDI and HMI line-of-sight magnetograms; tilt angles computed via flux-weighted polarity centroids; magnetic twist estimated using the force-free parameter α_best from HMI vector magnetograms; flare productivity measured by the flare index (FI) from GOES C-class-and-above events.
AI.result: Most ARs comply with the three empirical laws. Compliance rates are higher for ARs at higher latitudes or with larger centroid distance; total unsigned flux has limited influence except for Hale's law. FI shows no significant differences across compliance groups, except Cycle 24 ARs satisfying Hale's and Joy's laws but violating HHR show higher FI. Empirical thresholds for centroid distance and total unsigned flux effectively separate flare-productive from flare-quiet ARs.
AI.conclusion: Flare productivity of ARs does not depend on compliance with empirical polarity/tilt/helicity laws, but is more closely tied to sufficiently large and strong magnetic systems.

title: Beryllium enhancement in stars of the accreted Thamnos-2 system
summary: Surveys of Galactic halo stars have revealed numerous streams and substructures tracing stellar populations accreted by the Milky Way. Among these, Gaia-Sausage-Enceladus (GSE) and Sequoia are the most prominent, both associated with dwarf galaxies accreted about 10 Gyr ago. We aim to measure beryllium abundances in nine stars associated with Thamnos, a substructure possibly linked to Sequoia, following the discovery of a Be-rich star BPM3066 by Monaco et al (2025). We used Gaia photometry and parallaxes to compute ATLAS9 model atmospheres. Synthetic spectra were generated with Turbospectrum and used with MyGIsFOS in single-model mode to analyze UVES high resolution spectra. Four new stars exhibit a significant beryllium overabundance. Moreover, the two known Be-rich stars, HD 106038 and HD 132475, are also found consistent with Thamnos membership. Thus, all currently known Be-rich stars appear associated with the Thamnos-2 structure. The Be enhancement is accompanied by elevated Si abundances, and we detect a correlation between Be and neutron-capture elements. No comparable Be-rich population is known elsewhere in the Galaxy, pointing to a rare enrichment event. The measured A(Be)/A(Li) excess ratio bears the imprint of spallation reactions, pointing to a highly energetic event in which fast CNO nuclei fragmented upon collision with the surrounding medium. The silicon overabundance is also consistent with a hypernova origin. Such an event may have rapidly enriched the surrounding gas to [Fe/H] about -1.5 explaining the relatively high metallicities of stars formed from this material despite their old ages (about 13 Gyr).

AI.tldr: This paper identifies that all known beryllium-rich stars in the Milky Way halo are associated with the Thamnos-2 substructure, exhibiting correlated Be, Si, and neutron-capture element overabundances consistent with a hypernova event about 13 Gyr ago.
AI.motivation: To measure beryllium abundances in stars associated with the Thamnos substructure, following the discovery of a Be-rich star BPM3066, and to understand the origin of Be enrichment in the Galactic halo.
AI.method: Gaia photometry and parallaxes were used to compute ATLAS9 model atmospheres. Synthetic spectra generated with Turbospectrum and MyGIsFOS in single-model mode were used to analyze UVES high-resolution spectra of nine stars associated with Thamnos.
AI.result: Four new stars show significant Be overabundance; two previously known Be-rich stars (HD 106038, HD 132475) are consistent with Thamnos membership. All currently known Be-rich stars are associated with Thamnos-2. Be enhancement correlates with elevated Si and neutron-capture elements.
AI.conclusion: The A(Be)/A(Li) excess ratio indicates spallation reactions from a highly energetic event (likely a hypernova) where fast CNO nuclei fragmented. Si overabundance supports a hypernova origin. This event enriched gas to [Fe/H]~-1.5, explaining the relatively high metallicities of ~13 Gyr old stars formed from this material. No comparable Be-rich population exists elsewhere in the Galaxy.

title: Successive Coronal Jets as Novel Facilitators for Filament Oscillation and Eruption
summary: Solar filament eruptions are central to coronal mass ejections and space weather, yet their triggering mechanisms remain a fundamental open question. In particular, the early-stage that drives a magnetic flux rope toward instability and its observable signatures are poorly understood. Here, combining multi-instrument observations, we report successive coronal jets impacting a filament, causing its gradual rise and oscillations with growing amplitude and period. When the filament reaches the height where the decay index exceeds the torus instability threshold, the rapid filament eruption commences. This filament eruption is reproduced by magnetohydrodynamic simulations, in which successive thermal jets disturb a stable filament in a magnetic flux rope and excite oscillations together with the eruption of the filament. As the filament rises to erupt, the restoring forces for the oscillation progressively weaken, which naturally leads to an increase of the oscillation amplitude and period. Our results demonstrate the growing oscillations as one of the observable precursors for filament eruptions, enhancing our ability to predict solar eruptions.

AI.tldr: This paper reports that successive coronal jets impacting a solar filament cause its gradual rise and growing oscillations. When the filament reaches the critical height where the torus instability threshold is exceeded, rapid eruption occurs. The process is reproduced via MHD simulations, demonstrating that growing oscillations in amplitude and period serve as observable precursors for filament eruptions.
AI.motivation: The triggering mechanisms of solar filament eruptions, which are central to coronal mass ejections and space weather, remain a fundamental open question. The early-stage dynamics driving a magnetic flux rope toward instability and its observable signatures are poorly understood.
AI.method: Multi-instrument observations combined with magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) simulations. Observations track filament rise and oscillations; simulations reproduce thermal jets disturbing a stable filament in a magnetic flux rope.
AI.result: Successive coronal jets impact a filament, causing its gradual rise and oscillations with growing amplitude and period. Rapid eruption begins when the filament height reaches the decay index exceeding the torus instability threshold. MHD simulations confirm this mechanism.
AI.conclusion: Growing oscillations (increasing amplitude and period) due to progressively weakening restoring forces are identified as observable precursors for filament eruptions, improving the ability to predict solar eruptions.

title: Collisionless and collisional kinetics of a plasma atmosphere with spatially and temporally intermittent heating at its base
summary: The solar corona exhibits a pronounced temperature inversion, with plasma temperatures increasing by nearly two orders of magnitude from the chromosphere to the corona. We investigate how spatially sparse and temporally intermittent stochastic heating at the base of the transition region shapes the temperature and density structure of coronal loops within a kinetic framework. Stochastic thermal boundary conditions and surface coarse graining are introduced. Analytical solutions are derived in the collisionless limit for heating-event time scales shorter or longer than the particle crossing time, and Coulomb collisions are incorporated through a reduced kinetic model describing the thermalization of suprathermal particles. In the short-time-scale regime, spatial filling factor and temporal intermittency combine into a single effective parameter controlling the suprathermal population, producing a transition region and a hot corona both within individual loops and after coarse graining. Collisions preserve this thermal structure while reducing the coronal density through progressive thermalization. In the long-time-scale regime, individual loops are nearly isothermal and the temperature inversion emerges only after coarse graining, depending solely on the spatial filling factor. Here, Coulomb collisions and optically thin radiative losses have only minor effects, while density and temperature profiles remain broadly consistent with coronal observations. These results show that sparse, intermittent heating naturally generates suprathermal particle distributions and reproduces the observed thermal structure of the solar corona within a kinetic framework, highlighting the different sensitivity of the two regimes to collisional effects.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how sparse, intermittent stochastic heating at the base of the transition region shapes coronal loop temperature and density structure using a kinetic framework, finding two regimes (short and long time-scale) that produce the observed coronal temperature inversion with different sensitivities to collisional effects.
AI.motivation: To understand the physical mechanism behind the solar corona's dramatic temperature inversion (chromosphere to corona, nearly two orders of magnitude increase) using a kinetic framework with realistic stochastic heating conditions.
AI.method: Introduces stochastic thermal boundary conditions and surface coarse graining; derives analytical solutions in the collisionless limit for two heating-event regimes (shorter/longer than particle crossing time); incorporates Coulomb collisions via a reduced kinetic model for suprathermal particle thermalization.
AI.result: Short-time-scale regime: filling factor and intermittency combine into a single effective parameter controlling suprathermal populations, producing transition region and hot corona both within loops and after coarse graining; collisions preserve thermal structure but reduce coronal density. Long-time-scale regime: loops are nearly isothermal, temperature inversion emerges only after coarse graining depending solely on spatial filling factor; collisions and radiative losses have minor effects.
AI.conclusion: Sparse, intermittent heating naturally generates suprathermal particle distributions and reproduces the observed solar coronal thermal structure within a kinetic framework, with the two regimes showing different sensitivity to collisional effects.

title: Long-term speckle interferometric monitoring of binary systems II: 2007-2025 positional measurements and improvement of orbits
comment: 15 pages, 4 figures, 3 tables, accepted for publication in the Research in Astronomy and Astrophysics
summary: This article is focused on the analysis of observational data and improvement of orbital solutions of 8 objects. The listed objects are members of a sample of approximately 300 nearby (d < 100 pc) multiple systems compiled for long-term monitoring at the 6-meter telescope of the Special Astrophysical Observatory of the Russian Academy of Sciences. Speckle interferometric observations were carried out from 2007 to the present, which corresponds to 19 years of monitoring of the systems. Therefore, the new measurements are more numerous than or comparable to those in the literature, which makes it possible to make the observation series more complete. A long observation series made it possible to cover from 20% to 70% of the phases of the orbital periods of the objects under study. In combination with previously published data, the percentage of orbital coverage by measurements ranged from 45% to 85%. As a result, 4 orbits were classified as "definitive", one as "good", 2 as "reliable", and one as "preliminary" according to a grading scheme suggested by W.I. Hartkopf et al. Despite the high grades of the orbital solutions, it is recommended to continue monitoring (in particular, to obtain periastron observations) for HIP 1055, HIP 2532, HIP 15633, HIP 19472 and HIP 20751 in order to derive the final orbital parameters. It is not possible to obtain an accurate orbital solution for HIP 20227 due to measurements whose assigned weight strongly influences the period value, and for HIP 21710(Aa,Ab) due to the long orbital period (Porb > 200 yr) and, consequently, the lack of observational data. An analysis and comparison of the mass sums and masses of components obtained by two independent methods and based on several parallax values were also carried out. In most cases, the best agreement is found between fundamental parameters calculated using Gaia parallaxes, if available.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes 19 years of speckle interferometric observations of 8 nearby binary/multiple star systems, improving orbital solutions using new measurements that cover 20-70% of orbital phases (45-85% with literature data). Four orbits are classified as "definitive," one "good," two "reliable," and one "preliminary." Continued monitoring is recommended for five systems; two others resist accurate solutions due to weighting issues or long orbital periods. Mass sums and component masses are compared using Gaia and other parallaxes.
AI.motivation: To improve orbital solutions of 8 nearby multiple star systems through long-term speckle interferometric monitoring (19 years) and to assess orbital classification grades, while comparing dynamical masses with independent parallax-based methods.
AI.method: Speckle interferometric observations from the 6-meter telescope at SAO RAS (2007–present, 19 years). Orbital coverage analysis, Hartkopf grading scheme for orbit classification, and comparison of mass sums/masses from two independent methods using multiple parallax values (including Gaia).
AI.result: 8 orbits classified: 4 definitive, 1 good, 2 reliable, 1 preliminary. Orbital coverage: 20-70% from new data alone, 45-85% combined with literature. Better agreement found using Gaia parallaxes for mass calculations. Two systems unresolved due to measurement weighting issues (HIP 20227) and long period >200 yr (HIP 21710).
AI.conclusion: Continued monitoring (especially periastron observations) is recommended for HIP 1055, HIP 2532, HIP 15633, HIP 19472, and HIP 20751 to finalize orbital parameters. Gaia parallaxes provide the best consistency for fundamental parameter determination.

title: Multi-Path Quasi-Periodic Fast-mode Propagating Magnetoacoustic Waves to Diagnose Coronal Magnetic Field and Flaring Core
summary: Quasi-periodic fast-mode magnetoacoustic waves are often detected during solar flare events, although they are not observed in every flare, due to observational signal-to-noise limits and differences in flare magnetic topology and energy release strength. These structures propagate along magnetic configurations and supply effective diagnostics for coronal magnetic environments and flaring regions. Periodic signatures seen in fast-mode QFP wave trains carry physical information about excitation processes and propagation conditions. These signatures support quantitative studies of flare cores and magnetic channel properties. This work focuses on a well-documented event involving two oppositely oriented QFP waves simultaneously excited by a GOES-class M6.0 solar flare that occurred in active region NOAA 11261 on August 3, 2011. These QFP waves can be categorized into broad and narrow wave trains, with the narrow one propagating along funnel-like loops and the broad one moving through the low corona. Observational results suggest that both broad-wave and narrow-wave QFP phenomena can be simultaneously triggered by a single flare eruption. This study also indicates that such multi-path QFP wave events can be utilized to diagnose the magnetic field and the flare's core, and demonstrates the capability of multi-path QFP waves for robust coronal magnetic field and flare core diagnostics.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes a GOES-class M6.0 solar flare event on August 3, 2011 in active region NOAA 11261, which simultaneously excited two oppositely oriented quasi-periodic fast-mode magnetoacoustic (QFP) wave trains—a broad wave train moving through the low corona and a narrow wave train propagating along funnel-like loops. The study demonstrates that multi-path QFP waves can be used for robust diagnostics of the coronal magnetic field and flare core properties.
AI.motivation: QFP waves are not always detected in solar flares due to signal-to-noise limitations and variations in magnetic topology and energy release strength. Understanding their excitation and propagation provides quantitative diagnostics of flare cores and magnetic channel properties, motivating the need to study well-documented multi-path QFP wave events.
AI.method: The authors conducted an observational case study of a single M6.0 flare event using data from active region NOAA 11261 on August 3, 2011, analyzing simultaneously excited broad and narrow QFP wave trains and characterizing their propagation paths and properties.
AI.result: Both broad-wave and narrow-wave QFP phenomena can be simultaneously triggered by a single flare eruption. The narrow wave train propagated along funnel-like loops, while the broad wave train moved through the low corona, demonstrating multi-path QFP wave excitation.
AI.conclusion: Multi-path QFP wave events can serve as effective diagnostic tools for inferring coronal magnetic field properties and characterizing the flare core, offering robust capabilities for coronal magnetic field and flare core diagnostics.

title: Cataclysmic Variables Photometric Periods from TESS
summary: We present a sample of coherent and stable photometric period determinations for cataclysmic variables, based on TESS photometry through sector 102. We analyzed a total of 1557 cataclysmic variable stars and detected periodic variations in 1362 objects, including 286 eclipsing or ellipsoidal-variation systems, 63 polars, and 135 intermediate polars. In particular, we present the first determination of the optical variability period for 565 cataclysmic variables. Due to the 21x21" pixel size of TESS, we tested whether the variability was coming from the cataclysmic variable and not from a nearby star. For the intermediate polars, we detected spin periods in addition to orbital periods for 83 systems. We detect a clear period gap between ~2 and ~3 h in the eclipsing sample, consistent with previous work. The gap remains for the complete sample of photometric variability periods. There is no apparent gap in the orbital period distribution for intermediate polars. Finally, the median of the photometric period distribution of our complete sample of cataclysmic variables is 3.681 h. Comparing this to the distribution of rotation periods for likely single white dwarfs, we find a similar range, with a median photometric period of 6.803~h.

AI.tldr: This study analyzes TESS photometry across 102 sectors for 1,557 cataclysmic variables (CVs), detecting periodic variations in 1,362 objects. It provides first optical variability periods for 565 CVs, identifies spin periods in 83 intermediate polars, confirms the ~2–3 h period gap in eclipsing systems, and finds no gap for intermediate polars. The median photometric period for the full CV sample is 3.681 h, while the median for likely single white dwarfs is 6.803 h.
AI.motivation: To systematically determine coherent and stable photometric periods for a large sample of cataclysmic variables using high-quality TESS data, and to explore their orbital period distribution, period gaps, and population characteristics.
AI.method: TESS photometry from sectors 1–102 was used to analyze 1,557 cataclysmic variables. Periodic variations were detected via photometric period determination, with contamination checks against nearby stars given TESS's 21×21" pixel size. Eclipsing/ellipsoidal systems, polars, and intermediate polars were classified, and spin periods were additionally extracted for intermediate polars.
AI.result: Periodic variations detected in 1,362/1,557 CVs (286 eclipsing/ellipsoidal, 63 polars, 135 intermediate polars). First optical periods for 565 CVs. Spin periods detected for 83 intermediate polars. A clear period gap between ~2–3 h in eclipsing and full samples. No gap in intermediate polar orbital periods. Median photometric period: 3.681 h for CVs, 6.803 h for single white dwarfs.
AI.conclusion: TESS enables large-scale, robust period determination for CVs, confirming the classical period gap and revealing differences between CV subclasses. The median period distribution of CVs differs markedly from that of single white dwarfs, informing evolutionary relationships.
